title: Lands of Galzyr - Initial Thoughts
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=2bY_4Ut49J8

Daniel (@dungeondive) shares preliminary thoughts on Lands of Galzyr (a review copy), a gamebook/board-game hybrid that landed serendipitously right after he revisited Tales of the Arabian Nights — and reads as a more robust, more readable cousin to it. You play adorable animal-folk meeples roaming an open world, picking up side quests with no overarching plot or villain; the joy is simply wandering and living these characters' lives. Skill checks use a clever system where each skill pip swaps in a coloured die and adjacent skills lend a die too.

What excites him most is the campaign architecture: choose co-op, solo or competitive per session, each with a set number of in-game days; the world *remembers* your decisions across games via a save system, while characters persist. A real-time-meets-game-time calendar (with paired tokens marking when events, mounts and timers trigger) is a highlight. He walks a sample turn — move, draw a location/event passage from the online storybook, intimidate his way through helping a bullied young serval, earn prestige, pick up a new quest. There's lots of reading, but unlike Middara it never smothers the play. A charming open-world tease; full review to follow.

----

Lands of Galzyr has no main plot or villain — it's pure open-world wandering and side quests, a board-game Skyrim. Does a game with no central goal appeal to you, or do you need an objective pulling you forward?
